mnemonics.title: Leaf vs. Root Dominance
mnemonics.content: Visualize the recursion tree. The leaves represent n^(log_b a) work. The root represents f(n) work. Case 1: Leaves are heavy/wide (dominate), so T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a)). Case 3: Root is heavy (dominates), so T(n) = Theta(f(n)). Case 2: They are equal in weight, so T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a) * log n).

mnemonics.title: The 'a' and 'b' Song
mnemonics.content: a is the 'A'mount of subproblems (proliferation), b is the 'B'reakdown factor (division). The critical exponent is log base b of a. If f(n) grows slower than n^(log_b a), leaves win. If f(n) grows faster, root wins.

mnemonics.title: Polynomial Difference
mnemonics.content: The Master Theorem only works for polynomial differences. If f(n) is asymptotically smaller or larger by a factor of n^epsilon (where epsilon > 0), we have Case 1 or 3. If they are roughly the same (within a polylog factor), we have Case 2.

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity function for input size n
acronyms.usage: The running time of the algorithm we are analyzing.
In MergeSort (a=2), we split into 2 subproblems. In Binary Search (a=1), we look in 1 subproblem.
The factor by which the input size shrinks. In MergeSort (b=2), size becomes n/2.
The time spent outside the recursive calls (e.g., merging sorted arrays in MergeSort).
Used to define the polynomial gap. In Case 1, f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)). In Case 3, f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)).

visualTips.visualization: Draw the recursion tree as a triangle. The root is level 0. The width of the triangle at the bottom is the number of leaves (a^(log_b n) = n^(log_b a)). Visualize the 'work' as the area of the triangle. If the area is concentrated at the bottom (many small leaves), it's Case 1. If concentrated at the top (heavy root), it's Case 3. If distributed evenly, it's Case 2.
Graph n^(log_b a) (the balanced term) and f(n). Case 1: f(n) curve is below and flatter. Case 3: f(n) curve is above and steeper. Case 2: The curves run parallel (the gap is polylogarithmic).

For MergeSort T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n: Draw the tree. Level 0 has 1 node (work n). Level 1 has 2 nodes (total work n). Level 2 has 4 nodes (total work n). There are log_2 n levels. Total work is n * log n. This is the balanced case.

analogies.concept: Case 1: Leaf Dominance (e.g., T(n) = T(n/4) + n)
analogies.analogy: Think of assembling a massive Lego structure. Splitting the instructions (f(n)) is very fast (just tearing one page), but putting together all the tiny pieces at the leaves (n^(log_b a)) takes forever. The total time is determined by the assembly.

Think of coordinating a meeting between all employees. Dividing the list (subproblems) is easy, but calling everyone to join the call (f(n)) takes a huge amount of time (overhead). The total time is determined by the overhead.

Reading a book. You spend time turning pages (dividing/combining) and time reading the content (processing subproblems). These costs are roughly the same at every level, and there are 'log n' chapters to read. Total time is pages * chapters.
